region_of_origin: China (Northern Chinese cuisine, popularised across East and Southeast Asia)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for basi pingguo (Chinese candied apple). FOODON_03301710 (apple-based food product) is used as the closest parent. A more specific term for deep-fried caramelised apple dessert does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Cooked apple products) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for deep-fried battered caramelised apple dessert in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for basi pingguo or Chinese candied apple dessert. SNOMED CT does not cover this level of culinary specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of basi pingguo is approximately 200 g, comprising several battered and caramelised apple wedges served on a shared plate, intended for 2–3 diners.

nutrient_source.title: Basi Pingguo (Candied Apple) — estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Apple raw (FDC ID 1750340), Sugar granulated (FDC ID 1983674), All-purpose flour (FDC ID 1983706), Whole egg raw (FDC ID 748967), Sesame seeds (FDC ID 170150), Vegetable oil (FDC ID 171413); weighted recipe reconstruction at approximately 50% apple, 25% sugar, 12% flour, 8% oil absorbed, 3% egg, 2% sesame seeds by cooked weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for basi pingguo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted recipe reconstruction using USDA FoodData Central component foods. The dish consists of apple wedges coated in a light batter, deep-fried, then coated in hot caramelised sugar syrup. Oil absorption is estimated at approximately 8 g per 100 g cooked product based on standard deep-frying absorption factors for battered fruit. Values represent a plausible central estimate and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: Glucose, fructose and sucrose content of fruits and the glycemic index of apple; analogous estimate based on deep-fried battered apple with caramelised sugar coating referenced against Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283 — apple (raw) GI=38; sugar (sucrose) GI=65; composite estimate weighted toward high sugar content yields GI approximately 65
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for basi pingguo (Chinese candied apple). The GI is estimated as approximately 65 (medium) based on the dominant glycemic driver being caramelised sucrose (GI 65 per University of Sydney GI Database) combined with a minor contribution from apple flesh (GI 38, raw apple, University of Sydney GI Database). The batter and deep-frying process moderately slow glucose release, but the high sugar coating dominates. A composite weighted GI of 65 is defensible and conservative. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (65 × 43.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 56.6 g GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content; minimal cardiovascular sodium burden
hypertension.guidance: Basi pingguo contains approximately 18 mg sodium per 100 g, posing a low direct sodium burden for hypertensive individuals; however, portion size should be moderated given the high caloric density from sugar and frying oil.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (30.2 g/100 g) and medium-high glycemic index (65) with high glycemic load per serving (56.6)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Basi pingguo is contraindicated for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high free sugar content from caramelised sucrose coating, a medium GI of 65, and a glycemic load of approximately 56.6 per standard serving, which will cause a significant postprandial blood glucose spike; consumption should be avoided or strictly limited to very small occasional portions under dietitian supervision.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content from deep-frying; high sugar intake linked to dyslipidaemia and triglyceride elevation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-frying process contributes approximately 7.8 g fat per 100 g including 1.1 g saturated fat, and the high free sugar content (30.2 g/100 g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and adverse lipid profiles; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basi Pingguo (拔丝苹果, Candied Sweet Apple) is a classic Northern Chinese dessert in which fresh apple wedges are coated in a light egg-and-flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crisp, then immediately tossed in a hot caramelised sugar syrup that sets into a glassy, thread-pulling (basi) coating on contact with cold water or ice. The name 'basi' (拔丝) literally means 'pulling threads' or 'spun sugar', referring to the dramatic golden sugar strands that stretch when the pieces are pulled apart at the table. The dish is served immediately after preparation to preserve the crisp caramel shell before it softens. Texturally, the exterior is crunchy and intensely sweet from the caramel, while the interior apple flesh remains tender and slightly tart, providing a pleasing contrast. Sesame seeds are commonly sprinkled on the caramel for added aroma and texture. Nutritionally, basi pingguo is energy-dense at approximately 248 kcal per 100 g, driven by a high carbohydrate content of 43.5 g per 100 g — predominantly free sugars at 30.2 g — and moderate fat from deep-frying at 7.8 g per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium-high), with a glycemic load of approximately 56.6 per standard 200 g restaurant serving, making it clinically significant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is low in sodium (18 mg/100 g) and provides small amounts of vitamin C (2.8 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (1.2 g/100 g) from the apple component. In Singapore, this dish is served as a shared dessert in Chinese restaurants and is popular at banquet-style meals. It is vegetarian and halal-compatible when prepared with vegetable oil.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for basi pingguo exists in any approved primary or secondary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted recipe reconstruction from USDA FoodData Central component foods. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dominant sucrose component from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct measured source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted recipe reconstruction from USDA FoodData Central component foods (apple, sugar, flour, egg, vegetable oil, sesame seeds) as no direct database entry exists for basi pingguo. Values carry medium confidence and should be replaced with measured data if a direct source becomes available. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component foods used for this reconstruction and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 based on the use of vegetable oil (non-hydrogenated) in standard preparation; confirmed as effectively zero per USDA FoodData Central vegetable oil entries. 4. GI is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the dominant glycemic driver being caramelised sucrose (GI 65, University of Sydney GI Database) weighted against apple flesh contribution (GI 38, raw apple, University of Sydney GI Database). 5. The dish is served by See Ya Tonight 今晚有局 (Singapore Level 1) on field survey data at SGD 23.40. 6. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific dish in FoodOn or FoodEx2 as of 2026-04. 7. Multilingual names follow Singaporean Chinese cuisine rules: English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all provided.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full nutrient profile via weighted recipe reconstruction from USDA FoodData Central component foods as no direct database entry exists.

Applied Singaporean Chinese cuisine multilingual naming rules: added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names.

Set trans_fat_g = 0.0 with justification (non-hydrogenated vegetable oil, confirmed per USDA).

Provided specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including quantitative GI and GL values in Type 2 Diabetes guidance.
